perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any matters arising out of or related to this Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of Nevada.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any matters arising out of or related to this Agreement will be governed by principles of equity and fairness.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the laws of the State of Nevada' with 'principles of equity and fairness' introduces ambiguity. While equity principles can inform legal interpretation, they do not replace statutory law. This creates uncertainty as to whether Nevada law applies or some subjective standard, making the contract potentially unenforceable under established legal precedent.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 104.1105 allows parties to agree to which state's laws govern their agreement. By specifying 'principles of equity and fairness' instead of Nevada law, this deviates from established contract law principles and makes it difficult to ascertain which laws should be applied.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NRS: CHAPTER 104 - UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE—ORIGINAL ARTICLES [Rev. 6/29/2024 2:58:54 PM--2023] CHAPTER 104 - UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE—ORIGINAL ARTICLES ARTICLE 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Part 1 Short Titles, Scope, Construction, Severability and Electronic Signatures and Delivery NRS 104.1101 Short titles. NRS 104.1102 Scope of Article 1. NRS 104.1103 Construction of Uniform Commercial Code to promote underlying purposes and policies; applicability of supplemental principles of law. NRS 104.1104 Construction against implied repeal. NRS 104.1105 Severability. NRS 104.1106 Use of singular and plural; gender. NRS 104.1108 Relation to Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act. Part 2 General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation NRS 104.1201 General definitions. NRS 104.1202 Notice; knowledge. NRS 104.1203 Lease distinguished from security interest. NRS 104.1204 Value. NRS 104.1205 Reasonable time; seasonableness. NRS 104.1206 Presumptions. Part 3 Territorial Applicability and General Rules NRS 104.1301 Territorial applicability; parties power to choose applicable law. NRS 104.1302 Variation by agreement. NRS 104.1303 Course of performance, course of dealing and usage of trade. NRS 104.1304 Obligation of good faith. NRS 104.1305 Remedies to be liberally administered. NRS 104.1306 Waiver or renunciation of claim or right after breach. NRS 104.1307 Prima facie evidence by third-party documents. NRS 104.1308 Performance or acceptance under reservation of rights. NRS 104.1309 Option to accelerate at will. NRS 104.1310 Subordinated obligations. ARTICLE 2 SALES Part 1 Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter NRS 104.2101 Short title. NRS 104.2102 Scope; certain security and other transactions excluded from this article. NRS 104.2103 Definitions and index of definitions. NRS 104.2104 Definitions: Merchant; between merchants; financing agency. NRS 104.2105 Definitions: Transferability; goods; future goods; lot; commercial unit. NRS 104.2106 Definitions: Contract; agreement; contract for sale; sale; present sale; conforming to contract; termination; cancellation; hybrid transaction. NRS 104.2107 Goods to be severed from realty: Recording. Part 2 Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract NRS 104.2201 Formal requirements; statute of frauds. NRS 104.2202 Final expression: Parol or extrinsic evidence. NRS 104.2203 Seals inoperative. NRS 104.2204 Formation in general. NRS 104.2205 Firm offers. NRS 104.2206 Offer and acceptance in formation of contract.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that the laws of Nevada will govern the agreement. The changed text replaces this with 'principles of equity and fairness'. NRS 104.1301 states parties have power to choose applicable law. By replacing the specific state law with a broader concept, this creates ambiguity and undermines the initial choice of law, creating a contradiction in enforceability and application.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that after the delivery of this Agreement it or he will execute and deliver such further documents and do such further acts and things as another party may reasonably request in order to carry out the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that after the delivery of this Agreement it or he will execute and deliver such further documents and do such further acts and things as another party may arbitrarily request in order to carry out the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'reasonably request' with 'arbitrarily request' fundamentally changes the obligation. The original text implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing. 'Arbitrarily request' removes this limitation, potentially allowing one party to demand unreasonable actions, which contradicts the implied covenant of good faith present in Nevada contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, recognized in Nevada law, requires parties to a contract to act honestly and in good faith. Allowing 'arbitrary' requests contradicts this principle, as it permits actions that are not based on legitimate business reasons or fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonably request' to 'arbitrarily request' removes the implied duty of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'observance of fair dealing standards.' The modified text, by allowing 'arbitrary' requests, permits actions that do not align with honest dealing or fair dealing standards, contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.

perturbation.original_text: If any provision of this Agreement or its application to any Person or circumstances is held to be unenforceable or invalid by any court of competent jurisdiction, its other applications and the remaining provisions of this Agreement will be interpreted so as best reasonably to effect the intent of the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: If any provision of this Agreement or its application to any Person or circumstances is held to be unenforceable or invalid by any court, the entire agreement shall be void.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause contains a severability clause. By explicitly stating that the 'entire agreement shall be void' if one provision is unenforceable, this contradicts the purpose of severability clauses, which preserve the remainder of the contract. Nevada law generally favors upholding contracts where possible, especially with severability clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: By negating the severability clause, the modified text creates a condition where a minor, unenforceable provision could invalidate the entire agreement. This is contrary to the principle of contract interpretation, which seeks to give effect to the parties' intent as much as possible and preserve the contract as a whole. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184 (referenced above) describes the severability of an illegal term of an agreement and describes situations where a part of an agreement can be removed while keeping the integrity of the agreement, and this is contradicted by the text.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of severability, which aims to preserve the contract's validity even if some provisions are unenforceable. The original text includes a severability clause, while the changed text states that the entire agreement becomes void if any provision is unenforceable. This undermines the intent to uphold the contract as much as possible. Although both scraped snippets are unusable, the Perturbation and Law Explanations highlight the contradiction between the principle of severability (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184) and the modified text.
